ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general lamb rib food product entry; the spiced stew preparation is not separately coded. Closest parent match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers cooked sheep/lamb meat broadly; no specific code for spiced stewed lamb ribs exists. Closest parent match used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for spiced stewed lamb ribs dish. Null retained to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of lamb ribs stew is approximately 200g, comprising 2–3 bone-in rib pieces with sauce, as commonly plated in a Singaporean Indian restaurant.

nutrient_source.title: Lamb Ribs — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record carries HPB provenance with confidence 0.95 and review_status reviewed. All per_100g values are taken directly from the HPB Foodo entry for Lamb Ribs (G00010792). Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for cooked lamb rib (NDB 17174) as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this item; see notes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for spiced stewed lamb ribs. GI estimated at approximately 30 (low) based on analogous mixed meat-and-vegetable stew entries in the University of Sydney GI Database. The dish has low carbohydrate content (6.29g/100g), predominantly from vegetables and spices, with protein and fat dominating the macronutrient profile, which suppresses glycaemic response. GL per 200g serving = (30 × 6.29 × 2) / 100 = 3.77, rounded to 3.8.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 308mg/100g; borderline-high for a stewed dish
hypertension.guidance: Sodium at 308mg/100g is borderline-high; individuals with hypertension should limit portions to 120–150g, request reduced-salt preparation, and pair with high-potassium vegetables such as spinach or sweet potato to partially offset sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate (6.29g/100g) and low sugar (1.57g/100g) with moderate protein supporting glycaemic stability
type2_diabetes.guidance: With only 6.29g carbohydrate and 1.57g sugar per 100g, this dish has a low glycaemic impact; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes may consume a moderate portion (150–200g) while avoiding high-GI accompaniments such as white rice or naan bread.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from lamb (~2.8g/100g) and sodium 308mg/100g contribute to cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Lamb ribs contain saturated fat (~2.8g/100g) and borderline-high sodium (308mg/100g); individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size to 120–150g, choose lean cuts where possible, and balance the meal with fibre-rich vegetables to mitigate lipid and blood pressure effects.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Red meat (lamb) is a hard RA disqualifier due to arachidonic acid (omega-6) content amplifying inflammatory cytokines
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Lamb is classified as red meat rich in arachidonic acid, which amplifies pro-inflammatory cytokines (TNF-alpha, IL-6); individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this dish and substitute with oily fish (salmon, mackerel) or skinless chicken as RA-friendly protein sources.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Lamb Ribs (Spiced Stew) is a Singaporean Indian-influenced dish consisting of bone-in lamb rib pieces slow-stewed in a fragrant potpourri of whole and ground spices, which may include cumin, coriander, cardamom, cinnamon, cloves, turmeric, and chilli, along with aromatics such as onion, garlic, and ginger. The slow-stewing process renders the meat tender and allows the spice-infused sauce to penetrate deeply, producing a rich, aromatic, and mildly spicy flavour profile with a soft, fall-off-the-bone texture. The dish is commonly served at Singaporean Indian restaurants as a main course for lunch or dinner, typically accompanied by rice, roti, or flatbread. Nutritionally, per 100g it provides 117 kcal, 8.19g protein, 6.39g fat, and 6.29g carbohydrate, with sodium at 308mg — borderline-high for hypertensive individuals. The low carbohydrate and sugar content (1.57g/100g) make it suitable for Type 2 Diabetes management when portion-controlled and served without high-GI accompaniments. The saturated fat content (~2.8g/100g) and red meat classification raise moderate cardiovascular concern and constitute a hard disqualifier for rheumatoid arthritis due to arachidonic acid content. The dish is halal and gluten-free in its traditional preparation.
common_user_queries: Is lamb ribs good for diabetes?
Can I eat lamb ribs if I have high blood pressure?
How many calories in lamb ribs stew?
What spices are in Indian lamb ribs?
Is lamb ribs suitable for rheumatoid arthritis?

confidence_reason: All core macros sourced directly from HPB Foodo Singapore database (G00010792) with review_status reviewed and confidence 0.95. Saturated fat estimated from USDA SR Legacy cooked lamb rib as HPB does not separately report this value. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database methodology. Overall confidence is high given the canonical HPB provenance for the primary nutrient profile.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (2.8g/100g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for cooked lamb rib (NDB 17174) as the HPB Foodo record for G00010792 does not separately report saturated fat. This is documented as an adapted value. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this specific spiced stewed lamb ribs preparation are not available in the HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026-04-16. 3. GI of 30 is an analogous estimate based on mixed meat-and-vegetable stew entries in the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for spiced stewed lamb ribs has been published. GL per 200g serving calculated as (30 × 6.29 × 2) / 100 = 3.77, rounded to 3.8. 4. The input description references 'grilled/roasted preparation' in the diabetes health_suitability reason, which is inconsistent with the dish description of 'stewed'; the stewed preparation is used as the authoritative cooking method per the dish description. 5. Cultural classification as Indian cuisine is preserved from the input record; the dish is a Singaporean Indian restaurant preparation.
